speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Brand, it is 1944, and you already know that your parents are gone. And you are hidden by a Ukrainian family. How long where you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there-- it wasn't 1944. It was 1943 they liquidated the ghetto, the Germans. They were still advancing in Russia. When I got word that they, like I said before, they liquidated the ghetto, I went to this family to the other village on the other side of the lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in, and we kissed each other. Said to them, nobody knows. I came about 12 o'clock midnight. Nobody-- farmers go to sleep 7:00, 8 o'clock in the evening. They get up at 6:00, 5:00, 6:00. And I had-- it's bad of me to say that I had a comfortable life compared with other Jews. Because there was a grandmother. Wonderful person. They're farmers. And there was the daughter. And there was the husband and Olga, the girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huzar. H-U-Z-A-R. Huzar. And the mother was the instigator. She arranged it, and the father didn't say anything. Her husband didn't say anything. But she was the one to arrange the whole thing. She was a skinny girl with tremendous, a lot of endurance. A woman 45-years-old at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them till the Russians came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was March 1944. 1944. I stayed in the cold room. The big room didn't have no heat, but I didn't care. And they had a milk collection point for the cooperative, milk cooperative. The law required, Polish law required cement floor for hygienic purposes and no heat. So there was no heat. And I washed myself. She brought a big dish. A big-- this with ice cubes. I washed myself to be clean. There was water and ice cubes. But I didn't care. And that's how I survived. Then the Germans came back. Retreating Germans. Retreating Germans-- it's already 1944. I stayed there from the 20th of May till March 1944. March or February 1944. End of February the Germans coming. And it happened so that they decided to make-- they were staying here. They are coming to this village for a few days or so because they were running away west. And they decided they need the office in this house where I was hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in the garret. On the garret. I was in the house by myself sometime. The whole house belonged to me. Now I had to go to the garret. She had a ladder-- and the woman. And there was a sergeant, a typist. And there were Germans placed in different houses. You know, I don't know. It was only three, four days. Three, four days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were two military men. A sergeant and a corporal. And they were heading the records of this unit. And I was sitting there. There was straw, and she carried for me food. The woman, Mrs. Huzar, upstairs carried food. As soon as the Germans turned around. And she-- and then she carried my natural needs, you know, urinal. You know, I'm a human being. And I was fed good. I was never hungry. She came to pick up dirty dishes, the urinal, delivered breakfast. Delivered sometimes no lunch but all the time dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can hear it. And the Germans talk, and I hear through the cracks in the garret every word. And it was after dinner, evidently. And the sergeant sit down on the porch smoking a cigarette. And a soldier, or a corporal, or a German passed by on the village street. Hello, hello, and they talked. And he said, did you have a good-- what did you eat for dinner? He say, talk some nonsense. And then he said, did you hear? They found a Jewish woman in a basement in the village hiding. So what did they do? He says, they called the Gestapo. They took her away to Brody. And then he says, you know, people talk in the village that your landlady also is hiding a Jew. So the sergeant says, no, that's not possible. We are there eight hours a day. How can he hide himself? And I hear every word. Every word. That's all. The sergeant went away. He didn't sleep there. He slept somewhere else. Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave a signal to my landlady, Mrs. Huzar. I say, I have to leave. I have to get out from here. So what do you mean you want to get out from here? I give her a report what they say. You know, a rumor goes around. Pretty soon-- Ah. Nonsense. So no because if you hide a Jew, you get killed and the Jew get killed. Or your whole family gets killed and the Jew get killed. It happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say, no, I'm going away. Where are you going? I'm going in the woods. Say, look. She started not crying but she started to beg me, stay. And never heard. She gets around. No, I don't hear anything. I say, I'm going out because your life is in danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: 6 o'clock in the evening it gets dark. It was March. And there was a pump, a water pump by the barn. And the Germans walk around. Here, there, you know, a solider. I went down. After dinner, the sergeant went away and the corporal went away. I sneaked through, you know, behind their house. And I lied down behind the pump. And there was a German walking. And as soon as he walked out there from the barn, from the pump I ran to the barn. And in the middle of the night, I left the barn. And there was a field, you know, land, and it's March. Mud, just mud. And across the distance to the woods are on a slope. That's there out there, the forest. And I walk in the forest. And I was tired because I had heavy shoes, boots. I was dressed very warm. I had all my documents with me. I always slept with my documents. And I walk around and walk around and walk around. There is snow. And there's a ditch, and there's snow on the bottom of the ditch. That's wonderful. I take the snow and eat the snow. So I wasn't thirty because thirst is worse than hunger. And the ditch-- since how the ditch was a trench, a military trench from the First World War. And there is a ditch. And this is diagonal, and this part is diagonal. And the snow is on the bottom, and I lay down to rest up a little. And there is snow. And I look around, and I close my eyes. And look around, and I rest a little bit more. And suddenly on the snow, I see yellow spots like somebody urinated. And I don't pay attention to it. Then I open the eyes. I look around more and more. Here and there. And there is smoke comes out from a little hole from the ditch on the other side. A smoke, a little smoke. And I look at the yellow spot. They're gone. They're gone. So I figured it out. A Jewish family or a Jewish man is hiding. There was a store in his village. Parness was the name of the Jewish family who had the store for years and years. They're village Jews. And I knew I have to get away because I can't ask for help. And I don't want to expose them. And they would not help me, or they would kill me. And I just climbed out from this ditch, and I just walked away a little further. Because the ditch was, you know, meandering. You know, this way and this way. I find myself a different place to lie down. Maybe I slept. I don't know. But I was rested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comes in the morning-- the whole night. In the morning, I walk on a little through village-- through the forest. Not a road, you know, a path. And I walk and walk. It's 11 o'clock in the morning. Suddenly I see a soldier or a Gestapo man with a gun. And a Ukrainian farmer walking right against in the opposite direction. And the soldier doesn't say a word. Takes out a machine gun from his shoulder. And I turn around, and I know he wants to kill me. And the only thing I think is, does it hurt when he gets me right in the neck or in the head? Does it hurt? And I ran in the forest on this little path. And I ran and ran and ran. He didn't run after me. He didn't shoot. Then I can say this forest is dangerous. I go back to the village. Cross the road to the field and I come to the school. The best building in the village is the school. Nicely built primary school for the village children. And there is an outhouse in the yard. I go in the outhouse and lock myself. How long can you sit in an outhouse? An hour, that's all. I didn't have to use it. I was sweating but that's all. I was wet. Wet. I had a warm 3/4 coat, and I had felt boots with galoshes. Felt boots. That's what the solider wear. That's was the Russians wear 20 below zero, 30 below zero. I sit in the outhouse. Finally I got sick of this outhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk into the school. No children, naturally, it's war. But that there are two teachers, two sisters. They live there in the school. They had an apartment. And knew these two teachers because they came to the office in the village when I was in business. And I came some time to the school and they gave me a cup of tea, we were sitting, talking, and so forth. Before I came to the school, I walked into the mayor's office. And it was evening already. In the evening-- and I came in there. They gave me also a cup of tea. And I couldn't-- I was so tired I couldn't sit in a chair. I sat on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these people know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I was a doctor there. A Jewish doctor. And then he couldn't help. I decided that I'm not going to ask him for help. And I don't tell them where I'm going. I said, OK, I feel much better. Good bye. And went to the school. And I knew the teachers, too, because I was there a long time. And when my musical career also they came to this restaurant. It was a nice restaurant and they not only dancing, and the orchestra, but food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell her, you know, I have nowhere to go. I want to go on your garret and sleep overnight, and early in the morning I go away. So she says, I would like to have you but I'm afraid because you know what happens. They'll kill me, my sister. If you want to stay here, we will go away. We'll leave the building all together. You know, I said, where are you going to go? We know a lot of people in the villages. They went and I went on the garret. And I slept there. I slept I don't know how long. Maybe half a day. Next to the school there was two sisters, seamstresses. Also patients. One was a patient coming into my-- I didn't do very much for them, but they wanted, what's the matter with me? I done this, it hurts. I say, OK, you will be all right. Do this. Village medicine, you know. Hot compresses. So I say, it was already two days. Because one night I spent in the forest. One night I spent on the garret in the school. So that's the third day. And I don't know if the Germans are there or if the Germans are not there. So I told the sister, the seamstress, some people were talking in the village that she was a prostitute. To that's an additional profession but it doesn't bother me. So I say, you go. Now I didn't have to. So are the Germans in the village? I didn't know anything. I was cut off. She says, they're gone. They're gone. Who is in the village? Nobody. I say, do me a favor. You go to the Huzar house. Tell theme to come and get me. Want to go back to my home. That's my home. Pretty soon Huzar come in. And he said, why did you run away? He chastised me. He says, nobody asked for you. Nobody come to inspect. Nobody. Nothing. I say, yeah, but I want to play it safe. Your life is at stake. And I was so scared. I said, I don't want to go through the village street. There is one street. I want to go through these orchards. I want to go through the orchards. So he said, sure. Orchards. So we went to the orchards and-- [CRYING] Mrs. Huzar said, the same thing. Say, better play it safe. And you know, next 5 o'clock in the morning she gave me to eat. And I lie down. I sleep. 5 o'clock she wakes me up. The Russians are here. We have to wash your shirt and you take a bath. She made plans for me. Say, I want to sleep. I don't want a bath. Say, what are you sleep in that? And pretty soon, three Russian soldiers. I had breakfast. Pretty soon three soldiers. Three Russian Uzbeks, you know, not Russians from the Russian army. Uzbeks, you know. Muslim came in. And they sit, and they made them tea. She got tea, you know. And they had dilapidated guns. It's not much of an army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I go. I say, I go to the outhouse. I have to go to the bathroom, outhouse. On the way from the outhouse, the Germans bombed. German airplanes came back and bombing the village. And all the windows fell out. They killed 30 people in the village. Before I entered the house, I had to lie down by the brick wall, and it was covered with the glass. They were counterattacking. Counterattacking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you see, a ha. They're counterattacking. I'm not going to get the Germans again. So as I was astute, I walked out there with my documents. And I go through the field again. And I see suddenly, a sharpshooter line. A Russian sharpshooter line that goes through the field with the guns. Like a line about a quarter of a kilometer or half a kilometer. Everybody was gunned down. First line of sharpshooters. And I go straight to them. So I say, where shall I go? I'm a refugee. I didn't tell them I'm a Jew. I'm a refugee running away from the German. So go back. Go back behind us. They were so friendly. They were-- some of them were drunk because they give them to drink. And then I see there is a small forest and a house. I go to this house. And this house was requisitioned by a few Russian officers. And they're cooking soup for themselves. So I say, I am Jewish and I run away from the Germans. What do you advise me to do? He said, go back behind the front. So I go back. I go back and back. Suddenly, I see a farmer evacuating his family. Snow-- what do you call? Not a wagon-- A cart.

speaker: interviewee
text: A cart? With a horse pulling his mother-in-law, and a child, and his wife, and he walks. Say, can I hold onto the cart and go with you? He said, sure. So we walked. I walked, and the farmer walks, and the family rides. And we walk, and walk, and walk a whole day. They need anything but who cares. I have won. I can't be with the Germans and be caught again. Finally we come to a little town. A village town, Pochayiv. There was a big monastery, Greek orthodox monastery, too. And so I came to this village and I want to-- so I go to the monastery. I found out where is the authorities? The Russians are there. Say, the monastery, the civilian population, the civilian authorities are in the monastery. And then I walk on the street, and I see a hospital, a military hospital. I walk in there. They will draft me anyway, the Russians. So I want to report to volunteer to the Russian army. I walk into the reception room. There's a soldier sitting and typing, writing. I say, I want to see the head of the commanding officer of the hospital. Say, he'll be here in a few minutes. I sit down in the chair. Suddenly, the commanding officer comes in, and I say, I want to volunteer. I'm a doctor. Out. He threw me out. Civilian. Military premises-- civilian. Out! I'm a mashugana, I'm crazy. So I walked out. So I walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go to the civilian authorities, and they grabbed me. And as soon as I say, I'm a doctor and I am running away. I'm a Jewish-- so, we need doctors. I'm appointing you the head doctor of the district. Yeah, but I am very hungry. How about some food? And my socks are wet. And I'm afraid I'll get pneumonia. Oh, we give you tickets to the coffee shop. Civilian people, workers, Russians. Only for Russians. We'll give you. You go there, and you can eat. I come in there. They gave me a bunch of tickets. 10 or 5 tickets, something. What do you want? I say, whatever you have. Give it all together. So oh, yeah, we have. So they give me a plate, a tin plate with water. Hot water and about 17 noodles floating there. And a spoon. So I ate up. I drank it up. Give me more. I drank out five plates of soup water. Hot water. It was supposed to be soup. It was maybe I got maybe 75 noodles altogether.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came back. I feel a little better, you know, hot soup. He says, I don't have any instruments. I don't have nothing. Oh, we have a midwife here. She will be your pal, your colleague. She said, we have a colleague here for you. A midwife. We will send for her. She be-- and gave me a room. There are a lot of rooms in the cemetery. In the-- not the cemetery but the monastery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty soon, the midwife comes out with a shawl, you know. And said, doctor, doctor. Oh, yeah. She says, you need instruments? I have a thermometer, and I have a stethoscope. But you have to have a nice meal first. And she took me to her house. Beautiful, nice home. Very nice built home. And I go to the washroom and the tub is full of something. So I say, what's in the tub? What's in the tub? That's moonshine. We make moonshine, you know. Moonshine from potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she gave me a good meal. I ate up half a chicken. I have my equipment. A thermometer and a stethoscope. And I'm open for business. And you come to visit-- she told me things about the village. It's a known place for the church or by the monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pochayiv. This is bigger than a village. Bigger than a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Russia or was that Poland at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was Russia. There was when the Germans were retreating. A digression. I came on the way to Pochayiv. We stopped. It was this farmer who was transporting his family in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know. Pillows, and this, and a horse. And we came to a different-- we came to a different little town. Podkamien. It was Poland before the war. In fact, I had a cousin there, a doctor in Podkamien. And I had a friend was a doctor. Went with me to school. Was in New York a doctor. He's two years-- I bet he retired because he was older than me, two years older. And we came in, and we were all tired. And we stopped in a farmer in Podkamien. We stopped at the farmer. In the farmer's home. And they took us in. And everybody was tired. We got food, and I slept on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle of the night, somebody knocked on the window and on the door. And she woke up, the landlady. And she said, who is it? What is it? Give me my laundry back. Give me my laundry back. A soldier-- not a soldier. Like a sergeant. I didn't wash it yet. He say, yeah, the Russians are-- the Germans are counterattacking. I have to take my laundry back. And we are running. We are evacuating the city, the town. And sure enough-- and I say it-- because they are counterattacking. The Germans. When I hear this, I pull up my shirt. I slept in my pants. I pull up my boots and I took off.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I took off, I go, I see the hospital evacuating hospital. Trucks are running. Everybody running. The Germans are attacking. And I cannot-- nobody would take me. And I walked. I walked the whole day in snow. My feet-- I got frostbite. That's when they came to Pochayiv, where the monastery was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Pochayiv in this hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day. The next day after I ate, I met the midwife, and I ate a good meal, I went to sleep in my room in the monastery. And we're ready for business. I didn't have one patient. So I go out to look around. And there is traffic. All kind of trucks and so so. And all of a sudden, I meet Jewish people who also survived. From the forest, from hiding, I don't know. And trucks are running to Krzemieniec. I found out, Krzemieniec. They want tell me, you know, somebody saw your cousin in Krzemieniec alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear this, I got a chauffeur. I say, take me to Krzemieniec. Where are you going? To Krzemieniec. Take, I'll go with you. I jumped in on the truck. There were four people more. And I got to Krzemieniec. I left my passport. They took away my passport. They hired me, but just to make sure that I am not going to run away, they took away my Russian passport. Because I had a Russian passport from 1939. I came to-- I knew where they lived because we visited before the war. And I walk very tired. I walked and thinking, I'd meet my cousin on the street except he had a beard. And he recognized me. I did not recognize him. Misha, Misha! My name in Russian is Misha.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, like a resurrection. Come on. There's no talking. Come on. We go to where he lived. We walk in there, and that's my wife. She is not Jewish. When the Russians were there, they were standing there to get in line to get a loaf of bread. She was standing in front of him. That's before they had in Krzemieniec a ghetto. And she was there, and he was standing behind her. And he start to talk Polish. To make the story short, they fell in love. And she dug herself a hiding place in the yard of their home. Their two brothers were anti-Semites.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop again for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
